disclaimer: FIXTURE_ID=transcript_relationship. This transcript is fictional. All content synthesized for evaluation purposes only and does not represent any real clinical encounter or patient.
full_text: Patient discussed ongoing conflict with their partner over household labor and emotional availability. Therapist introduced an 'I-statement' framework and proposed a weekly 20-minute check-in ritual. Patient agreed to draft one I-statement before the next conversation and propose the check-in to their partner.

segments.text: We had another fight on Sunday. Same one we always have — the dishes, or really, everything the dishes represent.
segments.speaker: Speaker 0

segments.text: Same one you always have. What do the dishes represent for you?
segments.speaker: Speaker 1

segments.text: That I'm doing the emotional labor of the household while he's doing his job and checking out at five. The dishes are a stand-in.
segments.speaker: Speaker 0

segments.text: That's a pretty specific theme — uneven emotional labor. Have you named that with him in those words?
segments.speaker: Speaker 1

segments.text: No. I accuse him of being lazy about the dishes and we go around on that for an hour.
segments.speaker: Speaker 0

segments.text: Okay. So the dishes become the surface — and neither of you ever gets to the thing underneath.
segments.speaker: Speaker 1

segments.text: Yeah. He gets defensive, I get shrill, we disengage. Classic.
segments.speaker: Speaker 0

segments.text: I want to introduce a tool. It's called an I-statement. The structure is: when X happens, I feel Y, because Z. It sounds formulaic but it consistently de-escalates conflict by moving from blame to experience.
segments.speaker: Speaker 1

segments.text: So instead of 'you never do the dishes,' it's like 'when the dishes sit for three days, I feel unseen, because it signals to me that the household isn't a shared project.'
segments.speaker: Speaker 0

segments.text: Exactly. That's a really good one on the first try. Notice how it actually says what you need instead of attacking him.
segments.speaker: Speaker 1

segments.text: He still won't like it, but at least it's more accurate.
segments.speaker: Speaker 0

segments.text: The other thing I want to suggest is a weekly check-in ritual. Twenty minutes, on a set night. Not during conflict — the point is preventive.
segments.speaker: Speaker 1

segments.text: Like scheduled relationship maintenance?
segments.speaker: Speaker 0

segments.text: Yes. Structured: each person shares one appreciation, one irritation framed as an I-statement, one request. Twenty minutes, no interrupting.
segments.speaker: Speaker 1

segments.text: Weekly check-in, twenty minutes, appreciation plus I-statement irritation plus request. Okay.
segments.speaker: Speaker 0

segments.text: Would you be willing to draft one I-statement about the dishes before your next conversation, and also propose the check-in to him?
segments.speaker: Speaker 1

segments.text: Yeah. I'll draft one this week. The check-in I'll pitch to him Friday.
segments.speaker: Speaker 0

segments.text: Great. Let's also flag for follow-up — you mentioned he checks out at five. We should come back to what his inner experience might be, rather than just yours.
segments.speaker: Speaker 1

segments.text: Fair. I've been pretty one-sided in how I frame him.
segments.speaker: Speaker 0

segments.text: That's useful awareness. We'll revisit that.
segments.speaker: Speaker 1

test_queries.query: What communication tool did we learn for fights with my partner?
What weekly ritual did the therapist suggest?
